skill.name: workflow-stop-design
skill.description: Design guidance for adding graceful stop behavior to FastGPT workflows with Redis state, API support, and tests.
skill.summary: Design graceful workflow stop handling with Redis, dispatcher checks, and API integration.
skill.author: labring

security_audit.summary: The static scanner reported many high and medium patterns, but review found they are false positives from Markdown code fences, inline code, and TypeScript design examples. No executable scripts, shell commands, prompt injection text, network exfiltration, or malicious intent were found in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backtick Examples
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged Markdown backticks as Ruby or shell execution. The cited locations are front matter, inline code, fenced TypeScript examples, and workflow diagrams in a design document.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown code fences or TypeScript examples in SKILL.md. No executable Ruby, shell script, or command invocation is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Relative Import Paths
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged relative import paths as path traversal. Lines 107 and 108 are TypeScript import statements for project modules, not filesystem reads or user-controlled path access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is limited to static TypeScript import examples. There is no dynamic path construction, file access API, or traversal of user-provided input.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern
low_findings.description: Static analysis reported weak cryptography on metadata, test declarations, schema fields, API description text, and test names. These lines do not call hash or encryption algorithms.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations contain plain documentation and TypeScript schema or test text. No MD5, SHA1, cipher, or password-hashing implementation was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Logging Context Is Not Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged line 368 as reconnaissance. The line is a documented log field for appId inside workflow error handling and does not collect host, user, network, or environment data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is part of a TypeScript logging example for an application identifier. No system discovery command, host inventory, or secret collection behavior is present.

content.value_statement: Long-running workflows need a clear way to stop without leaving stale runtime state. This skill provides a Redis-backed design for stop signals, dispatcher checks, API behavior, frontend choices, and tests.
actual_capabilities: Explains Redis stop-key naming, TTL behavior, and cleanup rules.
Provides TypeScript helper designs for setting, checking, waiting for, and deleting stop signals.
Identifies workflow dispatcher integration points for polling and node-level stop checks.
Defines a v2 chat stop API shape with authentication and response behavior.
Outlines frontend stop behavior and optional API notification flow.
Lists unit test scenarios for Redis stop state and concurrent stop requests.
limitations: It is a design document and does not modify a codebase by itself.
The guidance is specific to FastGPT-style workflow dispatch and Redis state.
HTTP request nodes may keep running until their current request completes.
Teams must adapt import paths and authentication details to their repository.

use_cases.title: Add Stop Support To Agent Workflows
use_cases.description: Use the design to add graceful stop behavior to workflows that currently continue after a client abort.

use_cases.title: Review Runtime Cancellation Architecture
use_cases.description: Use the Redis state model and timeout flow to review cancellation behavior before implementation.

use_cases.title: Create Test Coverage For Stop Signals
use_cases.description: Use the listed scenarios to validate stop flag creation, deletion, timeout handling, and concurrent calls.

prompt_templates.title: Summarize The Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Summarize this workflow stop design in simple steps. Include Redis state, API flow, frontend behavior, and cleanup.

prompt_templates.title: Map Repository Changes
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this design to list the files I should update in my FastGPT repository. Explain the purpose of each change.

prompt_templates.title: Check Edge Cases
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this workflow stop design for edge cases. Focus on Redis failures, concurrent stop requests, stale keys, and long-running nodes.

prompt_templates.title: Produce An Implementation Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Turn this workflow stop design into an implementation plan with ordered tasks, test cases, rollout checks, and rollback notes.

output_examples.input: Explain how workflow stop state is stored.
output_examples.output: The workflow stores a short-lived Redis key for the app and chat. If the key exists, the dispatcher should stop before running more nodes. The key is deleted when the workflow finishes or expires after the TTL.

output: Verify that setting a stop signal makes the workflow report that it should stop.
Verify that deleting the signal returns the workflow to normal state.
Verify that waiting for completion exits on cleanup or timeout.
Verify that duplicate stop requests do not create inconsistent state.

best_practices: Keep stop keys short-lived so failed cleanup cannot leave permanent runtime state.
Check stop state before starting each new workflow node.
Keep API responses predictable while logging timeout and cleanup behavior for operators.
anti_patterns: Do not rely only on client connection aborts to stop backend work.
Do not store permanent stop state for transient workflow runs.
Do not report a workflow as stopped without defining timeout and cleanup behavior.

faq.question: Does this skill execute workflow stop logic?
faq.answer: No. It documents a design and example implementation steps for another codebase.

faq.question: Why does the design use Redis?
faq.answer: Redis provides shared state that separate API and workflow processes can check quickly.

faq.question: What happens if Redis fails?
faq.answer: The design recommends continuing the workflow when stop checks fail, then logging cleanup errors.

faq.question: Does the stop endpoint guarantee immediate cancellation?
faq.answer: No. It stops new node execution quickly, but some active HTTP requests may finish first.

faq.question: Can the frontend stay unchanged?
faq.answer: Yes. The document says existing abort behavior can remain, with an optional stop API call for better backend signaling.

faq.question: What tests are most important?
faq.answer: Test stop signal creation, deletion, timeout waiting, concurrent requests, and normal behavior after cleanup.
